name: Cape Hedo
tips: The drive north from Nago through Yanbaru forest is as rewarding as the cape itself — watch for Okinawa rails crossing the road at dawn. The cape is completely exposed to wind and spray; a windproof layer is useful even in summer. No facilities at the cape — bring water.

name: Cape Manzamo
tips: The cliff-edge path is unfenced in parts — supervise children carefully. The elephant rock arch is most photogenic from the southern viewpoint, not the main viewing platform. Sunset here over the East China Sea is one of Okinawa's finest free experiences.

name: East Hennazaki Cape
address: 城辺東平安名崎, Miyakojima City, Okinawa
tips: Wild lily fields bloom February–April, turning the peninsula edges white and fragrant. The 2km walk to the tip feels like being on the prow of a ship — ocean on both sides the entire way. By rental car from Miyako city (30 min east); no public transport reaches the cape.

name: Gyokusendo Cave
tips: At 5km, this is one of Japan's longest limestone cave systems — the tourist route covers 890m of stalactite-filled passages. The cave maintains a cool 21°C year-round — a welcome break in Okinawa's summer heat. The adjacent Okinawa World village has traditional craft demonstrations; the habu snake show (included) is surprisingly compelling.

name: Himeyuri Peace Museum
address: 糸満市伊原, Itoman City, Okinawa
tips: Allow 90 minutes — the farewell letters and photos of student nurses who died aged 15–19 are deeply affecting. The actual cave where survivors sheltered is preserved on-site. Combine with the Peace Memorial Museum in Itoman (5 min by car) for a comprehensive day of Okinawa wartime history.

name: Irabu Bridge
address: 伊良部大橋, Miyakojima City, Okinawa
tips: Cycling across the 3.54km bridge at sunset is a Miyako Island bucket-list activity — bicycle rental in central Miyako city takes about 15 minutes to ride to the bridge entrance. Irabu Island on the far side has secluded beaches and sea caves worth exploring. The water visible from the bridge appears in five distinct shades of blue on a clear day.

name: Iriomote Island
opening_hours: Island accessible year-round; mangrove kayak and jungle tours: 9:00–17:00; advance booking essential
tips: Over 90% of Iriomote is jungle — the Iriomote wildcat (yamaneko) lives only here. Mangrove kayaking on the Urauchi River is the signature experience; book in advance as operators fill quickly. Snorkeling and diving around Kabira Bay coral gardens rivals the Maldives for clarity. Stay overnight — day-trippers from Ishigaki miss the island's real character.

name: Kabira Bay
opening_hours: Glass-bottom boats: 9:00–17:00 year-round; swimming prohibited in the main bay
tips: The no-swimming rule is strictly enforced to protect the black pearl oyster farms — this is what keeps the water so extraordinarily clear. The glass-bottom boat tour takes about 30 minutes. Visit early morning for the best light and fewest boats. Sunset at Kabira is extraordinary — the light on the islets turns golden against the blue water.

name: Katsuren Castle Ruins
tips: The panoramic views from the upper ramparts over Katsuren Peninsula and the Pacific are among Okinawa's finest. Less visited than Shuri Castle — often quiet even on weekends. The castle is linked to the warrior lord Amawari's dramatic 15th-century story. Combine with the nearby Kaichu-doro sea road and sea-flooded rice terraces at Chinen.

name: Kouri Island & Heart Rock
opening_hours: Island accessible year-round; bridge: always open; Heart Rock beach: always accessible
tips: The 1,960m toll-free bridge gives extraordinary views of turquoise water in both directions. Heart Rock is two naturally eroded heart-shaped stones on a small cove — arrive before 9am to have it to yourself. The island's beaches are among the clearest in Okinawa main island. Rent a bicycle in the village to explore the beaches on the far side.

name: Makishi Public Market
address: 牧志3丁目, Naha City, Okinawa
opening_hours: Mon–Sat 8:00–21:00; Sun 8:00–20:00 (hours vary by stall; upper floor restaurants open during market hours)
tips: The market was rebuilt in 2023 — modern building, completely intact energy. Buy raw fish, pork, or sea snake from stalls on the ground floor, carry it upstairs, and have any restaurant cook it for a small fee. The sea grapes (umi-budō), fresh mozuku seaweed, and pig's trotters (tebichi) are Okinawa market staples. Arrive before 11am for the freshest selection.

name: Nago Pineapple Park
tips: A surprisingly fun agricultural theme park built around Okinawan pineapple cultivation. The motorised cart tour through the greenhouse tropical garden is a highlight. The pineapple products — wine, jam, soft-serve — are genuinely good. Combine with the American Village at Chatan or Ocean Expo Park for a full northern Okinawa day.

name: Naha Kokusai Dori
opening_hours: Always accessible; shops: 10:00–22:00; restaurants and bars: 11:00–late
tips: The side streets off Kokusai-dori (especially Ichiba Hondori and Mutsumi-bashi Street) contain Naha's best local restaurants and awamori bars — the main street is more tourist-facing. Makishi Public Market is a 5-minute walk east. The Tsuboya pottery district is a 10-minute walk southeast. Evening is the liveliest time.

name: Nakijin Castle Ruins
tips: Nakijin has the longest castle wall in Okinawa — 1.5km of curved coral limestone walls winding along a hilltop. The castle blooms with cherry blossoms in January (Okinawa's cherry season is Japan's earliest) while mainland Japan is still in winter. The view north toward the East China Sea from the upper walls is outstanding.

name: Okinawa Churaumi Aquarium
opening_hours: 8:30–18:30 (last entry 17:30); closed first Wed and Thu of Dec
tips: One of the world's great aquariums — the Kuroshio Tank (35m wide, 10m deep) with whale sharks and manta rays is the centrepiece. The outdoor dolphin and manatee shows are included with admission. Book timed entry in advance during Golden Week and summer. Combine with the adjacent Ocean Expo Park (free, with Okinawa native habitats) for a full day.

name: Okinawa Peace Memorial Park
opening_hours: Park: always open; Cornerstone of Peace: always accessible; museum: 9:00–17:00 (20:00 in Aug), closed Tue (except Aug)
tips: The Cornerstone of Peace lists all 241,000+ names of those who died in the Battle of Okinawa — Japanese, American, Korean, and others together. The cliff-edge setting above the East China Sea amplifies the emotional weight. The museum is the most comprehensive account of the Battle of Okinawa in existence. Allow 2 hours minimum. Combine with Himeyuri Museum (15 min by car).

name: Shikinaen Royal Garden
tips: The second royal garden of the Ryukyu Kingdom (18th century) — a UNESCO World Heritage site with reconstructed pavilions, stone bridges, and a spring-fed pond in traditional Ryukyuan garden style. Far less visited than Shuri Castle. The garden is compact but beautifully maintained. Combine with Shuri Castle (15 min by monorail) for a full Ryukyu kingdom day.

name: Shuri Castle
tips: Shuri Castle was the palace of the Ryukyu Kingdom for 450 years and a UNESCO World Heritage site — the main hall (Seiden) burned in 2019 and is currently being rebuilt, expected completion 2026. The reconstruction process is visible and educational. The outer stone walls and peripheral buildings are fully accessible. The view of Naha from the castle hill is excellent.

name: Sunayama Beach
opening_hours: Beach: always accessible; best light: morning and late afternoon
tips: Arrive before 9am to photograph the natural rock arch without crowds — by 10am weekday visitors and by 8am on weekends the cove fills. The sand dune descent to the beach is part of the experience. Snorkeling around the arch base reveals clear water and reef fish. The natural arch appears at its most dramatic in the late afternoon light from the beach below.

name: Taketomi Island
opening_hours: Ferry from Ishigaki: 15 min (runs frequently); island accessible year-round; bicycle rental: from ¥300/hour
tips: The most immaculately preserved Ryukyuan village in Japan — white sand lanes, traditional coral-wall houses, and water buffalo carts. Rent a bicycle to circle the tiny island (8km) in 1.5 hours. The Kondoi Beach on the west side has some of the finest sand in Yaeyama. No cars are allowed in the village centre. Stay overnight to experience the village in morning quiet before the day-trip crowds arrive from Ishigaki.

name: Tsuboya Pottery District
opening_hours: District always accessible; Tsuboya Pottery Museum: 10:00–18:00, closed Mon
tips: The cobblestone Yachimun-dori lane has over 20 active pottery studios and galleries — far more intimate than the larger Yachimun-no-Sato village in Yomitan. The Tsuboya Pottery Museum (¥350) gives excellent context for 300+ years of Okinawan yachimun traditions. Buy shisa guardian lion pairs directly from the makers — prices are significantly lower than airport souvenir shops.

name: Yanbaru National Park
tips: This is the only habitat of the Okinawa Rail (yanbaru kuina), a flightless endemic bird. Drive slowly on forest roads at dawn or dusk — you may spot one crossing the road. Bring insect repellent; the subtropical jungle is dense and humid.

name: Yonaguni Island
tips: Japan's westernmost point sees the last sunset of Japan each evening — a real novelty. The island is famous for the offshore "underwater ruins" dive site and for hammerhead shark schools (Jan–Mar). Car rental is essential as there is no public transport.

name: Yonaha Maehama Beach
tips: Repeatedly voted Japan's best beach — the white sand stretches 7 km. The shallow turquoise water is ideal for paddleboarding and kayaking. Stay at a hotel directly on the beach; sunset from here with Kurima Island in the silhouette is unforgettable.

name: Zakimi Castle Ruins
tips: One of Okinawa's nine UNESCO World Heritage Ryukyu castles. The double-arch gate is the finest surviving example of Ryukyuan stone masonry. Visit at sunset — the gate frames the East China Sea beautifully. Far less crowded than Shuri Castle.

name: Zamami Island
opening_hours: Ferry from Naha: 50 min (high-speed) or 2 hrs (regular); beaches open 24 hrs
tips: Furuzamami Beach consistently ranks among Japan's most beautiful. Rent a bicycle to reach the quieter Ama Beach on the far side. Winter (Jan–Mar) brings humpback whale sightings from shore. The village is tiny — book accommodation months ahead for summer visits.
